{{Infobox religious building | name = Tomioka Hachiman Shrine
富岡八幡宮 | image = Tomioka Hachiman 2022 2.jpg | caption = Tomioka Hachiman in 2022 | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|40|18.31|N|139|47|58.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1627 ([[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 4) | date_destroyed = | location = Tomioka, [[:en:Kōtō, Tokyo|Kōtō, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tomioka Hachiman Shrine'''|富岡八幡宮|Tomioka Hachimangū}} is the largest [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] in [[Tokyo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/htmls/goyuisho.html |script-title=ja:富岡八幡宮：御由緒 |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Tomioka Hachiman Shrine |date= |language= Japanese}} [[File:Tomioka hachimangu10.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Fukagawa Matsuri|Fukagawa Matsuri]]]] ==History== [[File:Fukagawa hachimangū keidai.jpg|thumb|[[Ukiyo-e]] woodcut print of the shrine by [[:en:Kuniteru|Utagawa Kuniteru]], 1853.]] The shrine was established in [[:en:Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]] in {{start date and age|1627|paren=y}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9pePrUSs7kMC&dq=Tomioka+Hachimangu+1627+tokugawa&pg=PA75|title= Historical Dictionary of Tokyo|date= 18 February 2011|page=75|publisher= [[Rowman & Littlefield|Scarecrow Press]]|isbn= 9780810874893}} with reclamation of a [[shoal]]. [[Hachiman]], whom the shrine reveres, was also a local [[kami]] of the [[Minamoto clan]], thus the shrine received cordial protection by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine lost the cordial protection it had enjoyed during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It was, however, chosen as {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}} by the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]], despite being considered of inferior status relative to [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] and other major shrines which the government had provided. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[sn:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]]

On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine lost the cordial protection it had enjoyed during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[sn:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]]

Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[sn:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Tomioka Hachiman Shrine
富岡八幡宮 | image = Tomioka Hachiman 2022 2.jpg | caption = Tomioka Hachiman in 2022 | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|40|18.31|N|139|47|58.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1627 ([[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 4) | date_destroyed = | location = Tomioka, [[:en:Kōtō, Tokyo|Kōtō, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tomioka Hachiman Shrine'''|富岡八幡宮|Tomioka Hachimangū}} is the largest [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] in [[Tokyo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/htmls/goyuisho.html |script-title=ja:富岡八幡宮：御由緒 |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Tomioka Hachiman Shrine |date= |language= Japanese}} [[File:Tomioka hachimangu10.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Fukagawa Matsuri|Fukagawa Matsuri]]]] ==History== [[File:Fukagawa hachimangū keidai.jpg|thumb|[[Ukiyo-e]] woodcut print of the shrine by [[:en:Kuniteru|Utagawa Kuniteru]], 1853.]] The shrine was established in [[:en:Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]] in {{start date and age|1627|paren=y}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9pePrUSs7kMC&dq=Tomioka+Hachimangu+1627+tokugawa&pg=PA75|title= Historical Dictionary of Tokyo|date= 18 February 2011|page=75|publisher= [[Rowman & Littlefield|Scarecrow Press]]|isbn= 9780810874893}} with reclamation of a [[shoal]]. [[Hachiman]], whom the shrine reveres, was also a local [[kami]] of the [[Minamoto clan]], thus the shrine received cordial protection by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine lost the cordial protection it had enjoyed during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It was, however, chosen as {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}} by the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]], despite being considered of inferior status relative to [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] and other major shrines which the government had provided. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[sn:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]]

Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[sn:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tomioka Hachiman Shrine
富岡八幡宮 | image = Tomioka Hachiman 2022 2.jpg | caption = Tomioka Hachiman in 2022 | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|40|18.31|N|139|47|58.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1627 ([[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 4) | date_destroyed = | location = Tomioka, [[:en:Kōtō, Tokyo|Kōtō, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tomioka Hachiman Shrine'''|富岡八幡宮|Tomioka Hachimangū}} is the largest [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/htmls/goyuisho.html |script-title=ja:富岡八幡宮：御由緒 |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Tomioka Hachiman Shrine |date= |language= Japanese}} [[File:Tomioka hachimangu10.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Fukagawa Matsuri|Fukagawa Matsuri]]]] ==History== [[File:Fukagawa hachimangū keidai.jpg|thumb|[[Ukiyo-e]] woodcut print of the shrine by [[:en:Kuniteru|Utagawa Kuniteru]], 1853.]] The shrine was established in [[:en:Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]] in {{start date and age|1627|paren=y}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9pePrUSs7kMC&dq=Tomioka+Hachimangu+1627+tokugawa&pg=PA75|title= Historical Dictionary of Tokyo|date= 18 February 2011|page=75|publisher= [[Rowman & Littlefield|Scarecrow Press]]|isbn= 9780810874893}} with reclamation of a [[shoal]]. [[Hachiman]], whom the shrine reveres, was also a local [[kami]] of the [[Minamoto clan]], thus the shrine received cordial protection by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[:en:Shitamachi|Shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine lost the cordial protection it had enjoyed during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It was, however, chosen as {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}} by the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]], despite being considered of inferior status relative to [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] and other major shrines which the government had provided. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[sn:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]]

On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[:en:Shitamachi|Shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". It was, however, chosen as {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}} by the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]], despite being considered of inferior status relative to [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] and other major shrines which the government had provided. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[sn:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]]

Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q654417]] Wikidata name: Tomioka Hachiman Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tomioka Hachiman Shrine
富岡八幡宮 | image = Tomioka Hachiman 2022 2.jpg | caption = Tomioka Hachiman in 2022 | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|40|18.31|N|139|47|58.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1627 ([[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 4) | date_destroyed = | location = Tomioka, [[:en:Kōtō, Tokyo|Kōtō, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tomioka Hachiman Shrine'''|富岡八幡宮|Tomioka Hachimangū}} is the largest [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/htmls/goyuisho.html |script-title=ja:富岡八幡宮：御由緒 |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Tomioka Hachiman Shrine |date= |language= Japanese}} [[File:Tomioka hachimangu10.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Fukagawa Matsuri|Fukagawa Matsuri]]]] ==History== [[File:Fukagawa hachimangū keidai.jpg|thumb|[[Ukiyo-e]] woodcut print of the shrine by [[:en:Kuniteru|Utagawa Kuniteru]], 1853.]] The shrine was established in [[:en:Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]] in {{start date and age|1627|paren=y}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9pePrUSs7kMC&dq=Tomioka+Hachimangu+1627+tokugawa&pg=PA75|title= Historical Dictionary of Tokyo|date= 18 February 2011|page=75|publisher= [[Rowman & Littlefield|Scarecrow Press]]|isbn= 9780810874893}} with reclamation of a [[:en:shoal|shoal]]. [[Hachiman]], whom the shrine reveres, was also a local [[kami]] of the [[Minamoto clan]], thus the shrine received cordial protection by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[:en:Shitamachi|Shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine lost the cordial protection it had enjoyed during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It was, however, chosen as {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}} by the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]], despite being considered of inferior status relative to [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] and other major shrines which the government had provided. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[sn:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]]

On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[:en:Shitamachi|Shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine lost the cordial protection it had enjoyed during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It was, however, chosen as {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}} by the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]], despite being considered of inferior status relative to [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] and other major shrines which the government had provided. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q654417}}

富岡八幡宮 | image = Tomioka Hachiman 2022 2.jpg | caption = Tomioka Hachiman in 2022 | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|40|18.31|N|139|47|58.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1627 ([[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 4) | date_destroyed = | location = Tomioka, [[:en:Kōtō, Tokyo|Kōtō, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tomioka Hachiman Shrine'''|富岡八幡宮|Tomioka Hachimangū}} is the largest [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/htmls/goyuisho.html |script-title=ja:富岡八幡宮：御由緒 |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Tomioka Hachiman Shrine |date= |language= Japanese}} [[File:Tomioka hachimangu10.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Fukagawa Matsuri|Fukagawa Matsuri]]]] ==History== [[File:Fukagawa hachimangū keidai.jpg|thumb|[[Ukiyo-e]] woodcut print of the shrine by [[:en:Kuniteru|Utagawa Kuniteru]], 1853.]] The shrine was established in [[:en:Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]] in {{start date and age|1627|paren=y}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9pePrUSs7kMC&dq=Tomioka+Hachimangu+1627+tokugawa&pg=PA75|title= Historical Dictionary of Tokyo|date= 18 February 2011|page=75|publisher= [[Rowman & Littlefield|Scarecrow Press]]|isbn= 9780810874893}} with reclamation of a [[:en:shoal|shoal]]. [[Hachiman]], whom the shrine reveres, was also a local [[kami]] of the [[Minamoto clan]], thus the shrine received cordial protection by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[:en:Shitamachi|Shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine lost the cordial protection it had enjoyed during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It was, however, chosen as {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}} by the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]], despite being considered of inferior status relative to [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] and other major shrines which the government had provided. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q654417}}

Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q654417}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Tomioka-hachimangu]][[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]][[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]][[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]][[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]][[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]][[ja:富岡八幡宮]][[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]][[zh:富岡八幡宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q654417]] Wikidata name: Tomioka Hachiman Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tomioka Hachiman Shrine
富岡八幡宮 | image = Tomioka Hachiman 2022 2.jpg | caption = Tomioka Hachiman in 2022 | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|40|18.31|N|139|47|58.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1627 ([[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 4) | date_destroyed = | location = Tomioka, [[:en:Kōtō, Tokyo|Kōtō, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tomioka Hachiman Shrine'''|富岡八幡宮|Tomioka Hachimangū}} is the largest [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/htmls/goyuisho.html |script-title=ja:富岡八幡宮：御由緒 |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Tomioka Hachiman Shrine |date= |language= Japanese}} [[File:Tomioka hachimangu10.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Fukagawa Matsuri|Fukagawa Matsuri]]]] ==History== [[File:Fukagawa hachimangū keidai.jpg|thumb|[[Ukiyo-e]] woodcut print of the shrine by [[:en:Kuniteru|Utagawa Kuniteru]], 1853.]] The shrine was established in [[:en:Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]] in {{start date and age|1627|paren=y}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9pePrUSs7kMC&dq=Tomioka+Hachimangu+1627+tokugawa&pg=PA75|title= Historical Dictionary of Tokyo|date= 18 February 2011|page=75|publisher= [[Rowman & Littlefield|Scarecrow Press]]|isbn= 9780810874893}} with reclamation of a [[:en:shoal|shoal]]. [[Hachiman]], whom the shrine reveres, was also a local [[kami]] of the [[Minamoto clan]], thus the shrine received cordial protection by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[:en:Shitamachi|Shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine lost the cordial protection it had enjoyed during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It was, however, chosen as {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}} by the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]], despite being considered of inferior status relative to [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] and other major shrines which the government had provided. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q654417}}

In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q654417}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Tomioka-hachimangu]][[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]][[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]][[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]][[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]][[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]][[ja:富岡八幡宮]][[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]][[zh:富岡八幡宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q654417]] Wikidata name: Tomioka Hachiman Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tomioka Hachiman Shrine
富岡八幡宮 | image = Tomioka Hachiman 2022 2.jpg | caption = Tomioka Hachiman in 2022 | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|40|18.31|N|139|47|58.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1627 ([[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 4) | date_destroyed = | location = Tomioka, [[:en:Kōtō, Tokyo|Kōtō, Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tomioka Hachiman Shrine'''|富岡八幡宮|Tomioka Hachimangū}} is the largest [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/htmls/goyuisho.html |script-title=ja:富岡八幡宮：御由緒 |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Tomioka Hachiman Shrine |date= |language= Japanese}} [[File:Tomioka hachimangu10.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Fukagawa Matsuri|Fukagawa Matsuri]]]] ==History== [[File:Fukagawa hachimangū keidai.jpg|thumb|[[Ukiyo-e]] woodcut print of the shrine by [[:en:Kuniteru|Utagawa Kuniteru]], 1853.]] The shrine was established in [[:en:Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]] in {{start date and age|1627|paren=y}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9pePrUSs7kMC&dq=Tomioka+Hachimangu+1627+tokugawa&pg=PA75|title= Historical Dictionary of Tokyo|date= 18 February 2011|page=75|publisher= [[Rowman & Littlefield|Scarecrow Press]]|isbn= 9780810874893}} with reclamation of a [[:en:shoal|shoal]]. [[Hachiman]], whom the shrine reveres, was also a local [[kami]] of the [[Minamoto clan]], thus the shrine received cordial protection by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. On the other hand, the shrine was revered from the people of [[:en:Shitamachi|Shitamachi]], and familiar as "Hachiman of Fukagawa". During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine lost the cordial protection it had enjoyed during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It was, however, chosen as {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}} by the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Meiji government]], despite being considered of inferior status relative to [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] and other major shrines which the government had provided. On {{start date and age|1945|3|10|df=y|paren=y}}, the shrine was burned down during the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|bombing of Tokyo]]. On 18 March 1945, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], who was inspecting the burned area, visited here and received the explanation about the damage in precincts. Upon returning to his palace, the emperor described his impression to Hisanori Fujita, his [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Grand Chamberlains]], comparing the effects to the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1923 which he had seen when Crown Prince: {{quote|It is far more tragic feeling in case of this time. Concrete remains etc. pain my heart further. It is miserable. Grand chamberlain! Tokyo also became a burnt ground at last by this.}} After the war, two stone monuments to commemorate the visit were built in precincts. In June 2017, the shrine decided to leave the control of [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].[http://diamond.jp/articles/-/134148 神社本庁「恐怖政治」の実態、地方の大神社で全面戦争も]、週刊ダイアモンド、2017th year 7月5日。 On 7 December 2017, the [[kannushi|chief priestess]] of the shrine, Nagako Tomioka, was stabbed to death, reportedly by her brother Shigenaga Tomioka. The attacker's wife also took part in the attack according to the police, injuring the priestess' driver. Her brother later stabbed his wife to death before committing suicide. A bloodied sword and knives were found at the scene.{{cite news|work=BBC|date=7 December 2017|title= 'Samurai sword' attack leaves three dead at Tokyo shrine |access-date= 7 December 2017|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-42275875}} ==Sumo== [[File:Yokozuna Stone Tomioka Hachiman 2010 September.jpg|thumb|Yokozuna Stone (center) and other memorial stones related to sumo.]] Tomioka Hachiman Shrine is also known as the birthplace of {{nihongo|{{ill|Kanjin-zumō|jp|勧進相撲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin-zumō|qq=}}|勧進相撲}}, founded in 1684 and origin of the current [[:en:Sumo|professional sumo]]. {{cite web |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=1358 |title=Fukagawa Koto-ku Guide - Tokyo |accessdate=2008-08-12 |publisher= Japan Visitor |date= }} Two ''[[:en:honbasho|basho]]'' (Spring and Autumn) were held at the shrine every year under the permission of the shogunate, and ''[[:en:banzuke|banzuke]]'' and other major systems were created in this period. After almost eighty years, ''basho'' had been held also in other places in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (Tokyo), then [[:en:Ekōin|Ekōin]] came to hold all ''basho'' since 1833. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1868–1912), sumo strengthened the relation with [[Shinto]] to survive because of losses of supports from the shogunate and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''s, who lost power by the [[Meiji Restoration]], thus the Shinto shrine came to be valued further by sumo. In 1900 (Meiji 33), the stone monument to commend successive ''[[:en:yokozuna (sumo)|yokozuna]]'', the {{nihongo|Yokozuna Stone|横綱力士碑|Yokozuna Rikishi-hi}}, was built by [[:en:Jinmaku Kyūgorō|Jinmaku Kyūgorō]], the 12th ''yokozuna''. Now, the stone inscribed with the ''[[:en:shikona|shikona]]'' of all ''yokozuna'' until [[:en:Terunofuji Haruo|Terunofuji Haruo]], the 73rd ''yokozuna'', and "unrivaled ''rikishi''" [[:en:Raiden Tameemon|Raiden Tameemon]]. The shrine has many other stone monuments related to sumo. Thus, when a ''[[:en:rikishi|rikishi]]'' reaches the Rank of ''yokozuna'', a dedication in the form of ''[[:en:Dohyō-iri|Dohyō-iri]]'' is done at the shrine. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} * [http://www.tomiokahachimangu.or.jp/ Tomioka Hachiman Shrine official website] - {{in lang|ja}} {{Clear}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Sumo venues in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan destroyed during World War II]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Koto, Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1627]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Fukagawa]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Tokyo]] }} [[en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tomioka-hachimangu}} [[el:Ιερό Τομίοκα Χατσίμαν]] [[es:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[fa:معبد فوکاگاوا هاچیمان]] [[fr:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:富岡八幡宮]] [[pl:Tomioka Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:富岡八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q654417}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q654417}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]
